Days.Events: Morning flight from Arlanda, arriving into Rome's thick July heat in the early afternoon. The taxi driver navigated the chaos of the ring road with operatic confidence, gesturing at landmarks while changing lanes without looking. Hotel Savoy was central and elegant, with a lobby cooled to a blissful degree. Wandered out once the luggage was dropped — down the Via Veneto, into the Villa Borghese gardens, and then a long meandering route down toward the Trevi Fountain, which was as overwhelmingly crowded as expected but still somehow moving. Dinner at a small trattoria on a side street where nobody spoke English and the menu was handwritten. Cacio e pepe and a litre of house white.

To the Colosseum, via the Roman Forum — the old centre of the world, now a field of broken columns and tourists with selfie sticks, which somehow doesn't diminish it. Arriving at the Colosseum early was the right call: the light was still slanted and golden, and the first hour had a contemplative quality before the crowds thickened. Stood on the arena floor and tried to imagine the noise. Spent the afternoon at the Capitoline Museums, which turned out to be better than expected — the bronze Marcus Aurelius alone is worth the visit. Gelato on the steps of the Pantheon in the evening.

Crossed the Tiber and spent the morning in the Vatican — the queue moved faster than expected and the Museums were overwhelming in the best sense. The Sistine Chapel is smaller than you imagine and the ceiling more vivid. Emerged into St Peter's Square blinking in the heat. The papal audience had concluded earlier that morning and the square was thinning out, but there was still a festive atmosphere. Walked the length of Castel Sant'Angelo on the way back, then ate lunch by the river watching tour boats pass. A long, slow afternoon of doing very little with great contentment.

Flight home with a long stopover at Vienna Schwechat — eight hours, which is too long to simply wait but just enough to make something of. Left the airport and took the S-Bahn into the city, had schnitzel and a Grüner Veltliner at a Beisl near Naschmarkt, walked through the first district for a couple of hours and admired the Staatsoper from outside, then made it back to the airport with time to spare. The connecting flight north felt like a gentle re-entry into ordinary life.

748781.TripDescription: Rome in July: ruins, religion, and cacio e pepe — with a surprise schnitzel in Vienna on the way home.

Days.Accommodation: Varbergs Kusthotell, Nils Kreugers väg 5, Varberg
Days.Events: Arrived at Kusthotellet late in the evening after a long drive down the coast. The hotel sits right at the water's edge and even in the dark you could hear the Atlantic swell working its way in. Checked in, found the bar, and spent an hour with the group going over the forecast for the week — it looked promising. Wind from the southwest, swell building through Monday, waist-to-chest height by midweek. Went to bed early, grateful for the sea air.

Days.Accommodation: Varbergs Kusthotell, Nils Kreugers väg 5, Varberg
Days.Events: First morning in the water and it did not disappoint. Drove the boards north to Norra Näs just after breakfast, where a sandbar break was producing clean, peeling lefts. The water was cold enough that a 4/3 wetsuit felt wise. Spent three hours in the lineup, caught a dozen decent waves and one really good one that ran almost to the beach. Muscles not quite warmed up yet — every paddle felt earned. Pizza and beer at the hotel in the evening, boards rinsed and drying on the rack outside.

Days.Accommodation: Varbergs Kusthotell, Nils Kreugers väg 5, Varberg
Days.Events: The swell stepped up overnight and the forecast held. Hit Stenared at first light — a reef break that requires a bit more commitment but rewards it. The sets were head-high on the bigger ones and the crowd was thin. Caught the best wave of the trip around eleven: a long right-hander that let me set two clean turns before the section closed out. Came in feeling invincible. Napped in the afternoon and paddled out again briefly before sunset, just to be in the water.

Days.Accommodation: Varbergs Kusthotell, Nils Kreugers väg 5, Varberg
Days.Events: Arms tired but spirits high. Took the morning a little slower — a long breakfast and a walk along the beach to scout before committing to a session. Ended up at Tången in the afternoon where the swell had reorganised into a more manageable shape. An older guy in the lineup turned out to be a retired shaper who'd been surfing this stretch of coast for thirty years — spent half the session listening to him describe what it used to be like here in the nineties. Dinner at a fish restaurant in town: freshly caught flounder with brown butter.

Days.Accommodation: Varbergs Kusthotell, Nils Kreugers väg 5, Varberg
Days.Events: Final full day in the water. The swell was backing off but the wind had swung offshore, smoothing the faces out beautifully. A long morning session at Norra Näs again — felt like a fitting return. Said goodbye to the lineup around noon, rinsed gear and packed boards. The last afternoon was spent on the hotel terrace in the weak autumn sun, nursing coffee and talking about next year.

Early flight home from Gothenburg. The airport felt jarring after five days of sea air and sand underfoot. Boards checked in as oversized luggage, wetsuit still faintly damp in its bag. Landed before noon and took the bus into the city, arms still heavy with the week's paddling and a face pleasantly salt-roughened.

534ea7.TripDescription: Five days chasing the swell along the Halland coast — cold water, clean waves, and one perfect right-hander at Stenared.

Days.Events: Took the overnight train north from Stockholm and woke up somewhere north of Sundsvall with pines pressing against the window and low morning mist on the bogs. Got off at a small station, shouldered the pack, and started walking. The first day was a long approach through mixed forest — the trail well-marked but not crowded, most of the other hikers heading in the opposite direction. Pitched the tent by a lake just before the light went amber. Boiled water, made dinner, listened to the forest.

A harder day — the trail climbed steadily from the lake and spent most of the morning above the treeline on open fell, the wind cool and the views enormous. Stopped for lunch on a boulder overlooking a string of blue lakes in the valley below, eating crackers and hard cheese while a pair of ravens circled overhead. The descent in the afternoon was through a stream-cut valley choked with birch scrub, legs complaining by the time we found a flat spot to camp near running water. Washed in the stream. Cold but worth it.

Woke to rain on the tent fly — a soft, persistent Nordic drizzle that showed no sign of lifting. Packed up in waterproofs and walked for six hours through low cloud, the landscape reduced to the twenty metres of trail immediately ahead. Not unpleasant, actually: a different kind of attention, more inward. Passed a mountain hut around noon and stopped for hot coffee and a conversation with the caretaker, who'd been up there all summer. Cleared up briefly in the late afternoon, enough to dry the tent before dark.

The last full day on the trail dawned clear and cold — the clearest yet. Climbed to the highest point of the route in the morning: a bare summit with a small cairn and a 360-degree view that stretched to haze in every direction. Stood there for a long time. The descent took most of the afternoon and delivered us to the edge of the trail system by early evening, where a gravel road led toward the nearest village. Final camp on a patch of grass next to a slow brown river.

Hitched a ride with a farmer's truck to the nearest train station and caught the morning service south to Stockholm. The pack felt heavier on the platform than it had all week. Watched the mountains recede through the train window, eating the last of the trail mix and already wondering when to go back.

fcc94b.TripDescription: Four days on foot in the Medelpad highlands — fell, forest, rain, and a summit view that made it all worthwhile.

Days.Events: Flew to Kiruna in the morning — a short flight but one that crosses something invisible, the landscape below becoming progressively emptier and more elemental. Landed under a high white sky and took a taxi east to Jukkasjärvi. The Icehotel in summer is a peculiar thing: the permanent cold rooms still exist, kept frozen year-round by a refrigeration system, while the rest of the complex is more conventional. Spent the afternoon exploring Jukkasjärvi village and the old wooden church on the riverbank before checking in and being shown to a room that was, as promised, below freezing. The sleeping bag was rated to −40°C. Dinner by the fire in the main hall.

Woke up in a room made of ice, which does not get less strange the second time you open your eyes. The cold had a dry, almost clean quality to it, and stepping out into the corridor — slightly warmer, still sub-zero — felt like surfacing. Breakfast in the warm building was eggs and coffee that had never tasted better. A morning walk to the river, where the midnight sun had barely dipped below the treeline all night and the water was mirror-still. Flight back south in the afternoon.

6a17b2.TripDescription: A midsummer night spent in a room carved from ice, under a sun that never quite set.

Days.Events: Hit the road mid-morning with the car loaded and a podcast queued up for the drive. The motorway south was busier than expected — everyone seemed to be heading somewhere for Christmas. Crossed the bridge into Denmark around noon and stopped in Odense for a quick lunch at a café on the pedestrian street before continuing north to Aarhus. The apartment on Christiansgade was charming — high ceilings, original wooden floors, and a kitchen that smelled faintly of whoever had cooked there last. Went for a short walk along the harbour before dinner.

Christmas Eve in Hellerup as tradition demands. The family gathered at the usual house, and the living room felt smaller than remembered — more people, more children, more noise. The meal was a generous spread: the same dishes as always, lovingly made, with a few small variations debated at length. The tree was real this year and slightly too tall, its top bent against the ceiling. Games and singing after dinner, and the kind of evening where time slows down pleasantly.

A quiet Christmas morning with leftover rice pudding and strong coffee before packing up and saying goodbye. The drive home on the 25th is always peaceful — the roads nearly empty, the countryside flat and frosted. Crossed back into Sweden in the early afternoon and were home before dark, in time to light a few candles and spend the rest of the day doing nothing in particular.

4dca7d.TripDescription: A familiar Christmas ritual — a too-tall tree, a brimming table, and the good kind of family noise.

Days.Events: Flight to Schiphol landed midday into flat, grey Dutch light — the kind of sky that feels like a painting of itself. Took the train direct from the airport to Centraal and walked the rest of the way to the Bunk Hotel, which turned out to be a converted office building with an appealingly industrial feel. Spent the afternoon cycling along the canals — rented a slightly creaky bike from a shop near Vondelpark. The city in March has a quiet, unhurried quality that summer visitors never see. Stopped at a brown café for a Heineken and a bitterballen before walking back.

Took an intercity train south to Maastricht — about two and a half hours through the flat Dutch heartland, which slowly gained a few gentle contours as the Limburg hills appeared. The Kruisherenhotel is built into a fifteenth-century Gothic church; the nave is now the bar and restaurant, and the room had stone arches and church windows. Spent the afternoon walking the old city: the Vrijthof square, the Sint Servaasbasiliek, the steep lanes down by the Maas. The city feels distinctly un-Dutch, almost Flemish or even French. Dinner at the hotel: the space alone is worth the price.

Train back to Schiphol in the morning, arriving with comfortable time to spare. A flat white and a stroopwafel in the terminal, and then the flight north over the North Sea and across the Swedish coast. Home by late afternoon, slightly wistful and already thinking about where to go next.

f226a2.TripDescription: Canals, church-hotels, and bitterballen — a quiet winter roadtrip from Amsterdam down to medieval Maastricht.

Days.Events: Left home just after breakfast with the car packed to the roof with gifts and luggage. The drive through southern Sweden was grey but uneventful, and crossing the Øresund bridge always feels a little ceremonial — the water below was dark and choppy. Arrived in Copenhagen around three in the afternoon, found the apartment on Store Kongensgade without too much trouble, and spent the rest of the evening walking around the nearby streets, picking up wine and cheese from a small deli.

Christmas Eve in Hellerup with relatives — the kind of evening that stays with you. The table was set beautifully with candles and silverware polished to a shine, and the food came out in waves: herring to start, then roast duck with caramelised potatoes and red cabbage, and finally a rice pudding with a hidden almond. The children took over the living room after dinner while the adults lingered at the table over aquavit and stories from Christmases past. Walked home through the quiet streets well after midnight.

A slow and pleasant Christmas morning with leftovers and coffee before loading the car and heading north. The Øresund Bridge was almost empty — just a few lorries and the occasional family car heading home like us. Stopped briefly at a petrol station just over the Swedish border for terrible hot dogs that somehow tasted perfect. Back home by mid-afternoon.

012eb3.TripDescription: Three days of candlelight, aquavit, and roast duck — a proper Danish Christmas with the family.

Days.Accommodation: Hotel Burdigala, 115 Rue Georges Bonnac, Bordeaux
Days.Events: An early morning taxi to the airport and a connecting flight through Frankfurt, where the layover was spent mostly at a gate café nursing an overpriced espresso and watching planes taxi in the heat haze. The second leg dropped down through clear skies over the Gironde estuary — rows of vines visible even from altitude. Arrived at Bordeaux–Mérignac early afternoon and checked into the Burdigala, which turned out to be a genuinely lovely hotel: calm, understated, with a courtyard that blocked out the city noise entirely. Dinner at a brasserie around the corner — steak frites and a carafe of local red.

Days.Accommodation: Hotel Burdigala, 115 Rue Georges Bonnac, Bordeaux
Days.Events: Drove out to a château in the Médoc in the morning — low stone buildings surrounded by geometrically perfect rows of Cabernet Sauvignon vines stretching to the horizon. The guide walked us through the cellars, which were cool and dim and smelled of old oak, and then we tasted four vintages back to back. Bought two bottles of something far too expensive and didn't regret it for a second. Ate lunch on the terrace of a farm restaurant nearby — charcuterie, bread, and a rosé so pale it was almost clear.

Days.Accommodation: Hotel Burdigala, 115 Rue Georges Bonnac, Bordeaux
Days.Events: A longer excursion today — took the D936 south towards the Sauternes region. The landscape shifted subtly: smaller plots, more variation in the soil, a slightly humid stillness in the air. Visited a small family-run domaine where the owner walked us through the vineyard himself, pulling off a leaf here and there to explain the season's progress. The dessert wine tasting at the end was extraordinary — one glass of an older vintage that tasted of apricot and honey and something almost medicinal. Back in Bordeaux for the evening, strolled along the Quai des Chartrons.

Days.Accommodation: Hotel Burdigala, 115 Rue Georges Bonnac, Bordeaux
Days.Events: Last full day. Spent the morning at the Cité du Vin — an impressive building shaped like a decanter that houses a sprawling interactive museum about wine culture globally. Slightly touristy but genuinely interesting. Had a glass on the rooftop terrace with a view across the river. In the afternoon, wandered through the Saint-Pierre quarter and did a little shopping, then had a long farewell dinner at a restaurant near the Place de la Bourse. The waiter recommended something from Saint-Émilion that was exceptional.

Up early and a cab to the airport. The flight home felt too short — barely enough time to finish a croissant and leaf through the notes from the trip before the Scandinavian coast appeared out the window. Landed in the afternoon and took the commuter train home, a bag heavy with wine and a head full of vineyards.

497fff.TripDescription: Four sun-drenched days among the vines of Bordeaux — cellars, châteaux, and one truly unforgettable glass of Sauternes.
